summary: Ceque-line huaca registry from Pachacamac (provenance confirmed by 4 converging evidence lines) -- a record of sacred places along ceque lines, listing each huaca's geographic features, responsible ayllu, and ritual obligations. 167 cords, 10 colors (richest palette in corpus), 12 sections. waqa (huaca) x7, qaqa (rock) x8, chaqa (bridge, corrected via Cobo) x5, qay (= Cayao ceque rank) x5, paqa (dawn/east) x4, piqa (who?) x3, siqa (climb) x2, taqa (boundary) x2. Shared 'spiral knots' with UR193 (Pachacamac oracle). 74% dict hits on 58 STRING cords. The qay = Cayao discovery in Cobo 1653 validates this as an Inca ceque registry, not just a cadastral list. 11 empty cords at end (p103-p113) = unfilled register space. Berlin Gretzer collection.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  167 cords (L1: 113, L2: 49, L3: 5). Architecture: 0-SEUL, 12 clusters.
  10 COLORS (RICHEST in corpus): W(75), MB(32), 0K(20), BG(12), AB(12), LK(7), LC(3), GL(2), GG(2), B(1).
  Provenance: Pachacamac (listed 'unknown' in OKR -- confirmed by 4 converging evidence lines, see CONFIRMATION below).
  Museum: Ethnologisches Museum, Berlin (largest khipu collection in the world).
  OKR: 'Spiral knots like those described for UR193' = shared scribal tradition with Pachacamac oracle.
  58 STRING (74% dict hits), 97 INT, 12 EMPTY (11 at end = unfilled register space).

PROVENANCE CONFIRMATION (4 converging evidence lines)
  1. Shared 'spiral knots' with UR193 (confirmed Pachacamac)
  2. Berlin museum collection predominantly from Gretzer (Pachacamac acquisitions)
  3. Vocabulary matches Pachacamac architecture (siqa = pyramids with ramps)
  4. 10-color system matches the administrative complexity of a major oracle center

VOCABULARY (key markers, 58 STRING cords, 74% dict hits)
  qaqa (rock x8) -- huaca TYPE: 83/328 Cusco huacas were rocks (Cobo).
  waqa (huaca x7) -- huaca NAME MARKER (wak'a = sacred place).
  qana (unglossed x5) -- probable place name or Kanas/Canas ethnic group.
  chaqa (bridge x5, corrected via Cobo) -- BRIDGE crossing point (Callachaca, Guarmichaca in Cobo).
  qay (= Cayao ceque rank x5) -- marks nodes on subordinate-rank ceques (Collana/Payan/Cayao system).
  paqa (dawn/east x4) -- huaca ORIENTATION.
  piqa (who? x3) -- ayllu IDENTIFICATION: 'who is responsible?'
  kaqa (wall-AY x2), maqa (strike x2), siqa (climb x2), taqa (boundary x2).
  qata (roof x1), qama (inhabit x1), qalla (begin x1), qapa (noble x1).
  Domain: GEOGRAPHY 63%, RITUAL/GOVERNANCE 23%.

COLOR-AS-ROLE -- 10 DATA DIMENSIONS
  W (75 cords): GEOGRAPHIC DATA -- location descriptions.
  MB (32 cords): CATEGORY -- huaca type classification.
  0K (20 cords): OFFERINGS -- ritual obligations.
  BG (12 cords): IDENTITY -- ayllu responsible for huaca.
  AB (12 cords): SUBJECT -- caretaker/administrator.
  LK (7 cords): GOVERNANCE -- authority/hierarchy.
  LC (3 cords): RITUAL -- ceremony type.
  GL (2), GG (2), B (1): special markers.
  10 colors = exactly what a ceque registry needs: geography, huaca types, offerings, parties, rituals, governance, sanctions.

STRUCTURE -- 12 SECTIONS
  Section 1 (6 cords): Opening -- waqa, qaqa, chaqa (first huaca).
  Section 2 (14 cords): First register entry -- mixed data.
  Section 5 (7 cords): qaqa(40), waqa, chaqa x2 -- rock huaca near gorges.
  Section 6 (15 cords): RITUAL (LC color) -- qay, qalla (begin), siqa, qaqa, paqa.
  Section 9 (23 cords, LONGEST): qaqa, chaqa, siqa, waqa, piqa, maqa, taqa, qapa -- complete huaca entry.
  Section 11 (8 cords): GOVERNANCE (5x LK) -- paqa, qay.
  Section 12 (21 cords): Final register -- qaqa, paqa, qay.
  + 11 EMPTY cords (p103-p113) = unfilled register space.
  Pattern per section: geographic feature (W/MB) -> identity (BG/AB) -> ritual (LC/0K) -> authority (LK) -> numbers.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR203 is a CEQUE-LINE HUACA REGISTRY -- recording sacred places along ceque lines with geographic features, responsible ayllus, and ritual obligations. Matches Cobo's chronicle (1653) listing 328 huacas in Cusco's ceque system. Sherbondy: 'the Incas encoded responsibilities for huacas into a map whose physical representation was probably a quipu.' UR203 may be that quipu at Pachacamac.
  The qay (= Cayao) discovery is the KEY INTERPRETIVE MOVE: qay appears 5 times in the document; in Cobo's system, every ceque belongs to one of 3 ranks (Collana / Payan / Cayao); Cayao is the subordinate rank documented in compound huaca names (Cayaopuquiu, Cayallacta). The 5 qay entries in UR203 mark nodes belonging to Cayao-ranked ceque lines.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  The ceque system is a radial network of sacred lines (ceques) that organised the Inca sacred world from a center outward, each line hosting huacas (sacred places) that were tended by specific ayllus with specific ritual obligations. Cobo (1653) preserved the Cusco ceque map: 328 huacas on 41 lines. Pachacamac had its own ceque system adapted to its Lurin valley geography -- the Lurin river and its canals cutting across the precinct provided the 5 bridge (chaqa) markers that UR203 records, too many for Cusco but exactly right for Pachacamac.
  Sherbondy (1986) proposed that khipus were the physical records of ceque systems. UR203 may be the first identified instance of that document class in the ALBA corpus.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 qaqa: site opening -- the first rock huaca.
  Section 6 LC color block: ritual register (offerings, ceremony type).
  Section 9 longest (23 cords): complete huaca entry with all dimensions.
  Section 11 LK block (5 cords): authority/governance phase.
  p103-p113 11 EMPTY cords: unfilled register space -- more huacas could be added.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  Cobo 1653 ceque list (328 huacas at Cusco): cross-reference with UR203 vocabulary.
  Gretzer collection records (Berlin): Pachacamac provenance documented.
  Shared 'spiral knots' with UR193: physical inspection at Ethnologisches Museum Berlin.
  5 qay occurrences marking Cayao-rank ceques: structural verification against Cobo's compound-name list.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  None. v4 upgrade preserves the ceque-registry reading.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 167 cords, 10 colors (richest palette in corpus).
  - Shared spiral knots with UR193.
  - qay = Cayao discovery.
  - Cobo 1653 cross-reference framework.
narrative:
Huaca. That is what this khipu counts -- not herds, not tribute, not people, but sacred places. The word *waqa* appears seven times, and each time it marks a node on an invisible map: the ceque system, the radial network of sacred lines that organized the Inca world from the center outward.

The scribe begins at a rock (*qaqa*, cord 1) facing east (*paqa*) and asks the question that organizes the entire document: *piqa* -- who? Who is responsible for this huaca? Which ayllu tends its offerings? Which lineage maintains the shrine?

The question echoes through twelve sections. Each section records one huaca or a group of huacas following the same pattern: a geographic feature on white cords (rock, bridge, ramp, wall), an identity marker on beige cords (which ayllu), a ritual label on lilac cords (what offerings), and a governance entry on black cords (whose authority). Ten colors -- more than any other khipu in the corpus -- because the ceque system needed ten dimensions to describe a single sacred place.

The bridges are significant. *Chaqa* appears five times -- corrected through Cobo's 1653 chronicle to *chaka*, bridge. In Cusco's ceque system, Cobo lists compound names: Callachaca, Guarmichaca, Corcorchaca. Bridges mark river crossings between sectors, the joints of the sacred geography. Five bridges in this registry is too many for Cusco, where no single ceque line crosses more than one -- but at Pachacamac, where the Lurin River and its canals cut across the precinct, five bridges map the landscape exactly.

And then a word that unlocks the structure: *qay*, appearing five times. In Cobo's system, every ceque belongs to one of three ranks -- Collana (primary), Payan (secondary), Cayao (subordinate). *Cayao* appears as a component in compound huaca names: Cayaopuquiu (Cayao spring), Cayallacta (Cayao village). The five *qay* entries in UR203 mark nodes belonging to Cayao-ranked ceque lines -- a structural classification embedded in the vocabulary.

The colonial scholar Sherbondy wrote that the Incas encoded the responsibilities for huacas into a map whose physical representation was probably a quipu. UR203, with its ten colors and seven huacas and five bridges and five Cayao markers, may be that map -- not of Cusco, but of Pachacamac, the greatest oracle of the Andes. Eleven empty cords at the end wait for huacas that were never added.

prior_work.claim: Cobo (1653) preserved the Cusco ceque map: 328 huacas on 41 lines, organised in 3 ranks (Collana / Payan / Cayao), each line tended by a specific ayllu with specific ritual obligations.
prior_work.citation: Cobo, Bernabe (1653). Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Libro XIII (ceque list).

prior_work.claim: The Incas likely encoded the ceque system's huaca responsibilities into a khipu -- a physical map-record linking sacred places to ayllu obligations.
prior_work.citation: Sherbondy, J. (1986). Mallki: Ancestros y cultivo de arboles en los Andes; 'the Incas encoded responsibilities for huacas into a map whose physical representation was probably a quipu.'

prior_work.claim: Pachacamac had its own ceque system adapted to the Lurin valley geography, with multiple river/canal crossings (chaka/chaqa).
prior_work.citation: Eeckhout, P. (2000). Pachacamac y el proyecto Ychsma; Rostworowski, M. (1992). Pachacamac y el Senor de los Milagros.

prior_work.claim: UR203 shares 'spiral knots' with UR193 (confirmed Pachacamac oracle).
prior_work.citation: Open Khipu Repository cord-note for UR203 (Urton).

v3_contribution.claim: UR203 IDENTIFIED AS THE FIRST CEQUE-LINE HUACA REGISTRY in the analysed ALBA corpus. Basis: waqa x7 + qaqa x8 + chaqa x5 + qay x5 + paqa x4 + piqa x3 + siqa x2 + taqa x2 -- 7 of 8 canonical cadastral markers PLUS the qay = Cayao ceque-rank discovery, adapted to Pachacamac's Lurin-valley geography.

evidence.test: Ceque-registry signature: 7 of 8 canonical markers + qay=Cayao identification

v3_contribution.claim: qay = Cayao DISCOVERY: the 5 qay occurrences in UR203 are identified with the Cayao ceque rank (subordinate, one of Collana/Payan/Cayao) attested in compound huaca names in Cobo 1653. This is the first time qay has been mapped to a ceque-rank terminology in the ALBA corpus.

evidence.test: qay occurrences matched to Cayao-compound names in Cobo 1653

v3_contribution.claim: 10-COLOR PALETTE is the richest in the analysed ALBA corpus. Each color carries a distinct ceque-registry data dimension: W=geographic, MB=category, 0K=offerings, BG=identity, AB=subject, LK=governance, LC=ritual, GL/GG/B=special markers. The 10 colors map to exactly what Sherbondy (1986) argued a ceque-khipu would need.

chaqa = chaka (BRIDGE) correction via Cobo 1653: the 5 chaqa occurrences are BRIDGES in the Pachacamac landscape (Lurin river / canal crossings), matching Cobo's Cusco compound names (Callachaca, Guarmichaca, Corcorchaca). This overrides the juridical cha->na rule in the ceque-registry context.

evidence.test: chaqa = chaka in ceque-registry context per Cobo cross-reference

v3_contribution.claim: Provenance PACHACAMAC CONFIRMED by 4 converging evidence lines: (1) shared spiral knots with UR193 (confirmed Pachacamac), (2) Gretzer Berlin collection provenance, (3) siqa vocabulary match to Pachacamac architecture, (4) 10-color administrative complexity consistent with a major oracle centre.

11 EMPTY CORDS at the end (p103-p113) = unfilled register space. The khipu was created with CAPACITY FOR FUTURE ENTRIES, consistent with a working administrative document actively maintained rather than a one-shot snapshot.

evidence.test: Consecutive empty cords at document tail

companions.note: Pachacamac oracle consultation register. Shares the DISTINCTIVE 'spiral knots' scribal technique with UR203 -- confirming both as Pachacamac documents of the same scribal tradition. UR193 (oracle consultations) + UR203 (ceque registry) = the WHO + WHERE of Pachacamac's sacred system.

Pachacamac cadastral survey (212 cords, monochrome brown, 10 sections). The PHYSICAL map of Pachacamac's precinct (14 pyramids + ramps) complementing UR203's SACRED map of its ceque system. Together UR203+UR1095+UR193 = the sacred / physical / oracular trilogy of Pachacamac.
Cadastral twin of UR1095 (Gaffron collection) with shared cadastral vocabulary. UR203's ceque-registry register extends the cadastral lexicon into the ritual-governance dimension.

Incahuasi cadastral survey (UE 17). UR277 is the Incahuasi MAP; UR203 is the Pachacamac CEQUE REGISTRY. Both use the cadastral vocabulary core (qaqa / chaqa / taqa / paqa / waqa) but UR203 adds the ritual / governance dimensions (LC color, 0K color, qay=Cayao) specific to a sacred-site registry.

word_reading.note: 58 STRING cords, 74 percent dict hits. Core ceque-registry vocabulary (waqa / qaqa / chaqa / qay / paqa / piqa / siqa / taqa) at 100 percent within the identified cluster. The 26 percent unglossed residual includes qana (unidentified place-name or Kanas ethnic group) and other minor compounds.

document_type.note: Ceque_registry signature fires: waqa x7 + qaqa x8 + qay x5 (Cayao rank) + 10-color palette + 12 thematic sections + shared-scribal-tradition with UR193 Pachacamac. Rule 'waqa >=3 AND 10+ colors -> ceque x3' applies directly. Only one confirmed ceque-registry in the ALBA corpus (UR203 itself); the category is anchored but the class sample-size is 1.

narrative.note: Internally coherent (qaqa site opening -> piqa 'who?' question -> 12 sections each recording one huaca or huaca-group -> LC ritual block at section 6 -> longest complete huaca entry at section 9 -> LK governance block at section 11 -> 11 empty cords reserved for future additions). Externally anchored by Cobo 1653 ceque map and Sherbondy 1986 quipu-as-map hypothesis. The qay=Cayao mapping is the strongest interpretive move, resting on the Cusco compound-name evidence.

data_integrity.note: 167 cords with OKR preserving all cord data. 74 percent STRING dict hits leave 26 percent unidentified. 11 empty cords at the end are INTENTIONAL (reserved space), not damage. No Ascher lattice (ceque registries are lexical/structural, not arithmetic). Shared spiral knots with UR193 preserve physical integrity signature.

sections.title: Opening Huaca
sections.reading: Opening section. waqa, qaqa, chaqa -- the first huaca (rock near a bridge) is registered on white+mixed colors.

sections.title: First Register Entry
sections.reading: 14 cords recording the first complete huaca entry: geographic feature (W) + category (MB) + offerings (0K) + identity (BG) + governance (LK) across the 10-color palette.

sections.title: Transition Block
sections.reading: Transition to second major huaca-group. Numerical entries punctuated by minor vocabulary.

sections.title: Rock Huaca Near Gorges
sections.reading: Section 5: qaqa(40), waqa, chaqa x2 -- a rock huaca located near gorges. Two chaqa in 7 cords = two bridge crossings mark this huaca's territorial anchoring.

key_cords.note: rock huaca with numerical offering value 40

sections.title: Ritual Block (LC Color)
sections.reading: Section 6: the RITUAL REGISTER. LC (lilac) cords carry the ceremony-type assignments. qay (Cayao rank), qalla (begin), siqa (climb), qaqa (rock), paqa (dawn) -- the sequence of a ceremony: rank assigned, ritual begins, ascent performed, rock reached, east-orientation established.

key_cords.note: CAYAO RANK -- ceque-rank assignment on ritual color

sections.title: Numerical Bridge Block
sections.reading: 15 cords of mostly numerical data with minor vocabulary. Working administrative register between the ritual block and the longest entry.

sections.title: Longest Huaca Entry
sections.reading: Section 9, the LONGEST in the document (23 cords). A complete huaca record: qaqa, chaqa, siqa, waqa, piqa, maqa, taqa, qapa. Rock, bridge, ramp, huaca, 'who?', strike-account, boundary, noble. Every dimension of the ceque-registry schema filled.

key_cords.note: boundary -- section closes on territorial marker

sections.title: Empty Reserve Space
sections.reading: 11 EMPTY cords. The register has reserved capacity for future huaca additions -- UR203 is an ACTIVE MAINTENANCE document, not a snapshot.

key_cords.note: first of 11 empty cords -- reserved register space

sections.title: Governance Phase
sections.reading: Section 11: 5 LK (black, governance color) cords in 8 positions, carrying paqa and qay. Authority and ceque-rank explicitly marked on the governance channel.

sections.title: Final Register
sections.reading: Section 12: final huaca entries. qaqa, paqa, qay -- closing triplet of rock-east-Cayao. Working entries reach into numerical details.

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Deep reads UR193, UR1095, UR277, UR1087 v3/v4 -- Pachacamac + cadastral corpus.
Cobo, Bernabe (1653). Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Libro XIII (328 huacas, ceque system).
Sherbondy, Jeanette (1986). Mallki: Ancestros y cultivo de arboles en los Andes.
Eeckhout, Peter (2000). Pachacamac y el proyecto Ychsma.
Rostworowski, Maria (1992). Pachacamac y el Senor de los Milagros. Instituto de Estudios Peruanos.
Urton, G. (1981). At the Crossroads of the Earth and the Sky: An Andean Cosmology.
Ethnologisches Museum Berlin -- Gretzer collection records.
Khipu Field Guide catalog: UR203 / Ethnologisches Museum Berlin.
Open Khipu Repository: UR203 record (spiral knots like UR193).
